title: Why participants can't skip identity verification in a workflow?
heading_path: Why participants can't skip identity verification in a workflow?
text:
Troubleshoot why Workflow Builder did not skip identity verification in a Send
Documents for Signature step.

Participants completed the Verify Someone's Identity step and
expected the system to skip re-verification in the subsequent Send Documents for
Signature step. However, the process required them to verify their identity
again.

Here are some potential reasons identity claims did not skip the
verification:

- The identity claim expired before participants reached the Send
  Documents for Signature step
- Participants used a different device or browser for the Send Documents
  for Signature step
- The participant’s name in the identity claim does not match the name in the
  envelope
- The ID Verification configuration for Send Documents for
  Signature differs from the one used in the original
  Verify Someone's Identity step

title: Why participants can't skip identity verification in a workflow?
heading_path: Why participants can't skip identity verification in a workflow?
The identity claim expired before participants reached the Send Documents for Signature step
text:
If the identity claim expires,
participants must complete a new identity verification.

title: Why participants can't skip identity verification in a workflow?
heading_path: Why participants can't skip identity verification in a workflow?
Participants used a different device or browser for the Send Documents for Signature step
text:
Participants must use the same device
and browser for both the Verify Someone's Identity and the
Send Documents for Signature steps. Identity claims only
apply if they do not change devices or browsers.

title: Why participants can't skip identity verification in a workflow?
heading_path: Why participants can't skip identity verification in a workflow?
The participant’s name in the identity claim does not match the name in the envelope
text:
Participants and process builders must
ensure name consistency. They must verify that the name used to generate the identity
claim exactly matches the recipient’s name in the envelope for the Send
Documents for Signature step.

title: Why participants can't skip identity verification in a workflow?
heading_path: Why participants can't skip identity verification in a workflow?
The ID Verification configuration for Send Documents for Signature differs from the one used in the original Verify Someone's Identity step
text:
Process builders must match the ID
Verification configuration. They must confirm that the Send Documents for
Signature step and the original Verify Someone's
Identity step use the same ID Verification configuration.

title: Agreement Process Steps
text:
This section provides detailed configuration procedures and requirements for individual workflow steps, helping process builders design automated, end-to-end agreement lifecycles.

This section contains detailed configuration information for key steps in the agreement
process.

[Configure a Collect Data with Web Forms Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=gua1698120920620.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to configure a Collect Data with Web Forms step in an agreement workflow. You use a web forms step to gather information from participants. You can use any existing stand-alone web form on your account that you have access to for your step.")
:   Learn how to design and configure web forms to efficiently gather necessary
    information from participants.

[Configure a Prepare eSignature Template Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=tskd83cc960-fb4b-4534-9573-1041591501e2.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to incorporate an eSignature template into your process, prefill recipient fields using upstream variables, and route completed documents to a secure repository.")
:   Use an envelope template to create a document to send for signature and map data from other workflow steps to template fields.

[Configure a Prepare Document Template Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=cra1749229692878.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to incorporate a document template into your process to generate customized agreements automatically using workflow variables.")
:   Use a document template to generate a document to send for signature.

[Configure a Verify Someone's Identity Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=qht1698126463108.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to configure a Verify Someone's Identity step in an agreement workflow. You use an identity verification step to verify a participant's identity using Docusign's ID Verification process.")
:   Understand the various methods available to verify participant identities,
    ensuring the security and validity of your agreements.

[Configure a Collect Data From an ID Data Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=huu1708131883761.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to configure a Collect ID Data step in an agreement workflow. You use this step to extract information from a participant's government-issued ID to autofill data in later workflow steps.")
:   Gather verified identity data for use in other workflow steps.

[Configure a Send Documents for Signature Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=uww1698167288211.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to add and configure a workflow step to send a document for signature.")
:   Explore the steps to use eSignature templates to obtain signatures and other
    information from workflow participants.

[Configure a Create Request Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=task-7234.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to add a Create Request step to a workflow to generate automatically or interactively a new Agreement Desk request.")
:   Generate a new Agreement Desk request automatically or interactively.

[Configure a Store Files in Google Drive Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=wqt1698167316926.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to configure a Google Drive storage step to automatically archive documents generated in earlier workflow steps.")
:   Save a copy of a workflow document to a Google Drive folder. Google is one of
    several apps that Workflow Builder supports for archiving files.

title: Installing Extension Apps for Workflow Builder
heading_path: Installing Extension Apps for Workflow Builder
text:
Learn how administrators locate, install, and authorize third-party extension applications from the Docusign App Center to use inside Workflow Builder.

In addition to Docusign apps, administrators can get extensions applications from the Docusign App Center. An extension app is a type of application that enables you to integrate
third-party application functionality into the Workflow Builder platform. You can include
workflow steps to trigger actions by an installed extension (such as Google Drive or
SharePoint), automating more of your agreement process.

Note: The HTTP app is listed on the App Center but does not need to be installed. Unlike third-party extensions that you must manually install from the Docusign App Center, the HTTP Web Request module is a **built-in** feature. It is automatically available for all accounts on IAM plans with Maestro add-ons.

You can install a variety of file storage and data write-back partner apps. More partner app
extensions will be added in future releases. As an administrator, you access the
[Docusign App Center](https://apps.docusign.com/app-center) and log in with your
Production or Demo Docusign account. You then install
and authorize extension apps for your account, enabling workflow creators to add
steps into a workflow that use the installed partner applications.

For more information for administrators on how to view, install, and manage partner extension
apps, see the [Docusign App Center
Guide](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=ous1698169987748&topicId=ijy1698170251179.html&_LANG=enus).

Note: Developers can review our extensive API documentation on building
extension apps for the Docusign App Center. See [Extension apps 101 overview](https://developers.docusign.com/extension-apps/extension-apps-101/) on the Docusign Developer
Center.

1. Log in to your Docusign account with account administrator credentials.
2. Navigate to the App Center in one of the following ways:
   - Open a new browser window, navigate to the appropriate environment for
     your account:
     - PROD <https://apps.docusign.com/app-center>
     - DEMO <https://apps-d.docusign.com/app-center>
   - Open the workflow designer view (create or edit a workflow) and select
     the App center link.
3. Review the available partner extension apps and install the apps you want to
   add to your account.

   For more information for administrators on how to view, install, and manage partner
   extension apps, see the [Docusign App
   Center Guide](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=ous1698169987748&topicId=ijy1698170251179.html&_LANG=enus).![Screenshot of the Docusign App Center.](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/extensionappmarketplace.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU2NDMsInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.BNtapZBcNUEObl2AD2gfsRG4hRyeGbOuOR5BxAcZrjU&_LANG=enus "Screenshot of the Docusign App Center.")

title: Update an Existing Workflow to Use a Start Method
heading_path: Update an Existing Workflow to Use a Start Method
text:
Learn how to update an older, published workflow by defining a required start method before saving and publishing new edits.

Older published workflows that do not have a start method defined are not impacted by
the new workflow start method. Any running instances for these workflows will
continue, uninterrupted. However, to update an existing workflow definition (or
when creating a new workflow), process builders will need to pick a workflow start
method. You cannot publish edits to an existing workflow until you add a workflow
start method.

![Screenshot of the start method options to update an existing workflow.](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/startmethodsupdate.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU2NjYsInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.J72FhEtDRr-vf-l0L7HKxyXlR8xk85w43_GIjGj7bT4&_LANG=enus)

You can read more about the start methods in this help topic [Workflow Start Methods](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=ztb1727892686033.html&_LANG=enus "Review the different automated and manual trigger methods available to initiate processes in Workflow Builder, including links, events, API calls, and CLM integrations.").

Depending on which method you want to use, and whether your workflow has starting
variables, the updates required for your workflow will differ. Some workflow start
methods no longer use starting variables

Review the following table for details on the Link, API Call, and CLM start methods.
The Manually in Docusign requires the more significant updates. Those details are
provided in the procedure following the table.

Tip: You can view your workflow's starting variables
by selecting either the From CLM or From an API
Call start method.

| Method / Starting Variables? | Example Uses | Update Process |
| --- | --- | --- |
| From a Link No variables | Participant opens a link shared with them, or embedded on an external website. | 1. Select the From a Link start    method. 2. Publish the workflow. |
| From an API Call - human start Yes  This start method preserves existing starting variables. | Participant starts a workflow from an external system using an API call with data for starting variables. | 1. Select the From an API Call start    method. 2. Select the Human option. 3. Add a participant step, such as a web form as the first    step. Assign it to the participant who will start the    workflow. 4. Publish the workflow. |
| From an API Call - automated process  Yes  This start method preserves existing starting variables. | External system starts a workflow using an API call with data for starting variables inputs. | 1. Select the From an API Call start    method. 2. Select the Automated Process    option. 3. If your workflow has any participant steps add a Set Up Invite before the first step so    that the participant can access the workflow. 4. Publish the workflow. |
| From Docusign CLM Workflow Yes  This start method preserves existing starting variables. | A CLM workflow starts a Workflow Builder workflow. Starting variables capture the incoming payload inputs. | 1. Select the From Docusign CLM start    method. 2. Publish the workflow. For more information on this start    method, see the topic [Enhance CLM Workflows with Workflow Builder Automation](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=hmz1727408977389.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how process builders trigger Workflow Builder processes from Docusign CLM to access advanced delivery options and signature features.") |
| Manually in Docusign  No variables | A web form step captures the variable information needed for the workflow. | (See the procedure following this table.) |

To use the Manually in Docusign  start method for a workflow
that has starting variables designed to collect information, you'll need to make several updates.

title: Update an Existing Workflow to Use a Start Method
heading_path: Update an Existing Workflow to Use a Start Method
text:
e the procedure following this table.) |

To use the Manually in Docusign  start method for a workflow
that has starting variables designed to collect information, you'll need to make several updates.

1. Create a stand-alone Web Form with all the fields needed to replace the
   starting variables used previously. Activate the web form.
2. Edit your workflow, and if your workflow starts with a Set Up
   Invite step, delete it.
3. Add an Collect Data with Web Forms step as the first
   step in your workflow. Use the web form you created to gather the information
   needed to start the workflow. Assign the step to the person who will start the
   workflow. If this person is not already a participant on the workflow, add them
   (e.g., "Preparer" or "Sender").
4. Add any additional steps you want the person starting the web flow to complete.
5. At the end of the starter's tasks, add a Confirmation
   Screen step to let them know they have completed all their
   assigned steps in the workflow.
6. If any of your existing steps rely on the original starting variables, edit
   those configurations and select variables from the web form added at the
   beginning of your workflow.
7. Edit the Start and select Manually in Docusign.

   You will get a warning that starting variables were created and will be
   deleted. Since you created a web form to capture this information, you can
   continue.
8. Publish the workflow.

The workflow is updated for a start manually from within Docusign. To launch this process, the preparer selects Run Workflow from the
Workflows list. The workflow takes them directly to the web
form to provide the information needed.

title: Workflow Migration
text:
Read about how Docusign has migrated older workflows to adapt to our most recent product enhancements. This update provides you with access to new features and improved functionality.

Docusign is continually advancing the capabilities and functionality of
the Workflow Builder platform. To ensure that your workflows can take advantage of our latest
releases, we are automatically updating your older workflows to the latest version.

title: Workflow Migration
text:
Docusign is automatically migrating older workflows to the newest format. In this process, we are creating a draft version of your published workflow and migrating it to the latest version. All draft workflows will also be migrated.

This important update allows us to provide you with the latest Workflow Builder features. For details on
the new functionality, see the [What's changing?](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?language=en_US&bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=con03a49b0b-428d-4a5e-a5f0-ae8408072abd.html&_LANG=enus&anchor=con03a49b0b-428d-4a5e-a5f0-ae8408072abd__title-25)
section in this topic.

Important:

Your existing published workflows will remain active and continue to function as
they did before the migration. This ensures that there is no interruption to
your live workflows. You can use the new draft version created by the migration
to explore the updates and new features.

When you edit a migrated draft, you will see a notification banner that says, "We've
migrated you to the latest workflow version. Learn More." To use the new features
and latest format, you must [review and publish](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=tskf086e141-baa5-4a34-bd8e-48989d01cbec.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to review and publish a draft workflow after Docusign automatically migrates it from an older format. When you publish the draft, that will activate the new features and replace the active older version.")
the migrated draft.

After the migration, the Last Updated date for a workflow will reflect when the updated draft was created or updated.

Note: Older workflows cannot be imported in their unmigrated state. To import a workflow, it must first be edited and migrated to the new version.

title: Workflow Migration
text:
The latest workflow format includes several changes to improve functionality and simplify the design process:

- **eSignature steps:** The Get Signatures step is now
  separated into two distinct steps. The original configuration settings will be
  retained and located in the appropriate new step.

  - Prepare eSignature
    Template. This step prepares a document to send for signature. This step uses an envelope template from your account. You can prefill recipient fields by mapping them to workflow variables.
  - Send Documents for
    Signature. You use this step to present a document to one or more participants to view, sign, or receive a copy. The documents you send come from either a Prepare eSignature Template or one or more Generate Document steps.
- **Participant information:** Participant data, previously contained in a single object (the Update Participant Info step), is now moved to the steps where it is needed.
- **Prepare Document Template step:** Create dynamic documents from a document template and data from a workflow instance.
- **Merging conditional paths:** Combine two or more paths in a branching rule to lead to a common outcome.
- **Suggested next steps:** Workflow intelligence provides a helpful list of suggested next steps to simplify the workflow design process.

title: Limits for Branching Rules
text:
This topic provides a detailed explanation of how branching limits work within the rule configuration for the Add a Branching Rule and Add a Multi Branch Rule steps in Workflow Builder. It covers specific limits and how path merging and counting logic affects the ability to add subsequent branch rules.

This topic provides a detailed explanation of how branching limits work for the two types of branching rules for adding simple True/False or complex multi-branching to your workflow. For general how-to information on setting up branching rules, see the topics [Configure a Branching Rule](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=mnv1698169910928.html&_LANG=enus "You can create conditional rules in your agreement workflow. These rules validate a condition and branch the workflow based on whether the condition is met or not.") and [Configure a Multi Branch Rule](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=xtg1749585007265.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to use the Add a Multi Branch Rule step in Docusign Workflow Builder to create workflows with multiple paths based on specific conditions. This feature allows for dynamic processes that adapt based on data collected from web forms.").

title: Limits for Branching Rules
text:
When you are building a workflow with branching rules, the system enforces a maximum of 8 branch rules per path. A path is an individual outcome of a branching rule, and includes all of the subsequent steps executed after that outcome is met. This limit can include a mix of both multi branch and simple True/False branch rules.

Of these 8 total branch rules, no more than 2 can be configured as Multi Branch Rules. (Allowed combinations are provided in the details that follow.) These limits apply only to the branching rule steps. Other types of steps added to the paths do not count towards the branching rules limits.

Maximum limit
:   You can add up to 8 branch rules on a single path.

Multi branch rule limit
:   Within the 8 total branch rules, you can configure a maximum of 2 Multi Branch rules.

Counting examples
:   - For an existing True/False branch rule, you can add up to 7 more branches on both the true and false paths; no more than 2 of these 7 branches can be Multi Branch rules.
    - For an existing Multi Branch rule, you can add up to 7 more branches to each path (rule outcome); only 1 of the 7 branches can be a Multi Branch rule.

Allowed combinations
:   - 2 Multi Branch Rules + 6 True/False Branches
    - 1 Multi Branch Rule + 7 True/False Branches
    - 0 Multi Branch Rules + 8 True/False Branches

Enforcement
:   For each single path in your workflow, the count is cumulative from the first branching rule on through to the end of the workflow.

    - If the path contains 8 branches, no additional branch rules can be added.
    - If the path contains 2 Multi Branch Rules, no additional Multi Branch rules can be added.

title: Limits for Branching Rules
text:
When you add a step within an existing flow, the system calculates the branch limit based on the longest possible sequence of branches leading to that point.

- Longest Path Measure: The limit is always measured against the longest possible path within the workflow structure.
- Impact of Merges: After two or more branches merge back into a single path, the logic must still account for all possible originating paths. This can restrict what subsequent steps or branches can be added post-merge. This ensures that every path leading to the merge point honors the branching rule limit.
- Pre-Branch Counting: The same counting logic applies to the structure preceding a new branch, ensuring the total path length remains under the 8-branch limit. In other words, the count begins at the first branch in a workflow and continues through to the end.

title: Limits for Branching Rules
text:
For a Multi Branch Rule, each of its possible outcomes is treated as an independent path for the purpose of the branch count.

- Independent Paths: Every path within a Multi Branch Rule—for all specific conditions met and the final "no conditions met" path—is considered separately when checking the branch count limit.
- Further Branching: An additional Multi Branch Rule (or other branch type) can be added on each of the specific paths generated by the current Multi Branch Rule, including the "no conditions met" default path. Maximums for total branch rules and multi branch rules apply.

title: Limits for Branching Rules
text:
Branching rules are complex. This section presents some examples that illustrate where branches can be added in a workflow and how the system’s path counting logic operates. The ability to add a new branch rule is determined by the path's current branch count and the overall structural complexity of the orchestration workflow at that specific point.

**Example: Workflow with the maximum allowed 8 branching rules.**

In this workflow, there is one maximized path with 8 simple True/False rules. The count from 1 to 8 is shown on each rule to show the counting logic, so “True / False count = 1” is the first rule, “True / False count = 2” is the second rule, and so on.

Adding branching rules to this workflow is limited as follows:

- No additional rules of either type can be added along the original True path in this workflow.
- No additional rules of either type can be added to the last branch where the count has reached the maximum allowed. (Labeled “True / False count = 8”)
- At the open paths (the “+” signs), rules of either type can be added up to the maximums allowed. For example, for the original False path, you could add 7 more True/False rules or a combination of up to 2 Multi Branch and up to 5 True/False rules.

![](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/max_branching_rules.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU4MzgsInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.P5D3TPqoZfwcLUlRf8W8S2HX9nL0y_JLfA-xFVs08YU&_LANG=enus)

**Example: Workflow with 3 branches**

In this workflow, there are a total of 4 branching rules: 3 True / False and 1 Multi Branch. The first three branches are configured to merge, leading to a single outcome. The two paths off of the initial True / False rule are each evaluated separately.

- Since only 2 Multi Branch rules are allowed in a path, before the final merge point, you can add 1 more Multi Branch rule to the right side (blue circle) and 2 more to the left side (red circle).
- Before the final merge point, you can add 5 True/False rules to either side (blue or red circles).
- At the final outcome, where all branches have merged together (green circle), you could add either 1 Multi branch rule; 4 more True / False rules; or 1 Multi branch and 3 True / False rules.

![](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/simple_merge.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU4MzgsInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.P5D3TPqoZfwcLUlRf8W8S2HX9nL0y_JLfA-xFVs08YU&_LANG=enus)

**Example: Linear workflow - no merging**

In this workflow, the initial Multi Branch rule does not merge and a variety of nested rules are in use. All configured paths have the maximum number of rules (8), meaning no more can be added at any point on those paths. The count from 1 to 8 is shown on each rule to show the counting logic, so "True / False count = 1" is the first rule, "True / False count = 2" is the second rule, and so on.

Adding branching rules to this workflow is limited as follows:

- No additional rules of either type can be added along the 3 paths that have the maximum number of branches.
- At the open paths (the “+” signs), rules of either type can be added up to the maximums allowed. For example, on the right-most path that originates with a Multi Branch Rule, depending on where you are in the rule chain, you could add up to 7 more True/False rules or a combination of up to 1 Multi Branch and up to 6 True/False rules.

![](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/complex-linear.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU4MzgsInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.P5D3TPqoZfwcLUlRf8W8S2HX9nL0y_JLfA-xFVs08YU&_LANG=enus)

title: Managing Multiple Documents in Workflow Builder Envelopes: Order and Limits
heading_path: Managing Multiple Documents in Workflow Builder Envelopes: Order and Limits
text:
Read about how to add, reorder, and combine multiple documents or templates in a **Send Documents for Signature** step, and learn about the limits for document types, quantity, and recipient mapping.

When sending documents for signature, the order of documents in an envelope matters. Customers often include multiple files where the sequence impacts readability and the signing flow.
You can **define the order of documents** in the Send Documents for Signature step to make sure your recipients receive files in the correct sequence.

As a process builder, you can add three types of documents to an eSignature step:

- **Prepared eSignature template**: The template roles are applied to the envelope as recipients.
- **Prepared document templates**: The template roles are applied to the envelope as recipients. If you add multiple document templates, any duplicated roles are consolidated. For example, you have two templates that both use "Signer 1". These roles are consolidated in the eSignature step so that only one "Signer 1" recipient appears.
- **Supporting documents**: These are extra files that do not contain recipient roles or fields for data input. Examples include Salesforce files and web forms attachments.

The step must include one "primary document". A primary document is either a prepared eSignature or document template. if you add multiple documents to the step, you can click and drag the files to rearrange the presentation order to ensure recipients receive files in the intended sequence.

title: Managing Multiple Documents in Workflow Builder Envelopes: Order and Limits
heading_path: Managing Multiple Documents in Workflow Builder Envelopes: Order and Limits
text:
This section includes important restrictions and limits you should be aware of:

- Only one Prepared eSignature template can be used in an envelope.

  - Prepared Document templates cannot be included with an eSignature template.
  - Up to 129 supporting documents can be included with an eSignature template.
- Up to 15 Prepared document templates can be added to an envelope. (1 primary generated document and 14 additional.)
- Up to 130 documents in total can be added to a Send Documents for Signature step. Examples:

  - 1 Prepared envelope template and 129 supporting documents.
  - 15 Prepared document templates and 115 supporting documents.
  - 10 Prepared document templates and 120 supporting documents
- Envelope size limits are subject to the existing eSignature limits as described in [this help article](https://support.docusign.com/s/articles/Limitations-When-Creating-or-Signing-an-Envelope-in-DocuSign).

title: Agreement Actions and Workflow-Powered Rules
text:
Learn how workflow-powered rules transform Agreement rules into automated processes using Workflow Builder, enabling richer, multi-step processes while maintaining trusted configurations.

For customers who use Agreement rules to archive or export agreements, Workflow Builder can take those existing rules and turn them into automation steps inside a workflow. This lets you keep using the rules you trust today, while expanding them into richer, multi‑step automations that run in Workflow Builder.

When you save an Agreement rule that uses an Archive to action, Docusign creates a Workflow Builder‑powered system workflow behind the scenes. You can then use that rule to generate an editable workflow that uses the rule's event as the trigger and its archive action as one of the workflow steps. Administrator permissions are required both to create rules and to use them as automation steps in a workflow.

title: Agreement Actions and Workflow-Powered Rules
text:
Workflow‑powered rules are Agreement rules that run on Workflow Builder behind the scenes.

When you save an Agreement rule that uses an **Archive to**, DocuSign creates and publishes a **system workflow** in Workflow Builder that represents that rule. This conversion happens automatically:

- The rule appears in the **Agreement Rules** list in the Agreement Actions Admin view, using the same configuration UI you use today.
- In the Agreement Rules list, these rules are labeled to indicate they are **powered by Workflow Builder**.
- The underlying system workflow:

  - **Does not** appear in your  **Workflows** list.
  - **Does not** count against your standard workflow allowance.

From an administrator's perspective, you still create and manage a "rule." Behind the scenes, Workflow Builder is now responsible for running it.

title: Agreement Actions and Workflow-Powered Rules
heading_path: Turning a rule into an automation step in a workflow
text:
For supported actions, Workflow Builder can use a workflow‑powered rule as the starting point for a richer, multi‑step workflow. You can add additional steps to the new workflow that starts with the completed envelope event specified by the agreement rule.

When you choose to add automation steps from an Agreement rule:

- Docusign creates an **editable user workflow** based on that rule.
- The workflow is configured with:

  - A **From an Event** start method for an envelope completed event. The event conditions match the rule's parameters. For example, the rule is designed to archive all completed envelopes sent by a specific email address. In the workflow start method, the event conditions specify that the sender's email must equal the address used in the rule.
  - A workflow step that performs the same **Archive to** action defined in the rule.

This new workflow appears in your standard  **Workflows** list and, once published, counts against your workflow allowance, as defined by your plan. Your original rule remains in the Agreement Rules list. The system workflow tied to that rule continues to run it, while the user workflow you created lets you extend that same logic into broader automations.

Note: Changes to a rule you use to create a workflow are NOT reflected in your workflow. The workflow uses a system-generated copy of the rule that cannot be modified. To make changes, edit your workflow details directly.

For detailed steps on this process, see the help topic [Create a Workflow from an Agreement Rule](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=tsk9166c067-1d6e-49a9-91c8-641cda5f7f65.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to generate a workflow from an Agreement Rule, using the rule's event as the automation trigger and its archive action as the initial workflow step.").

title: Agreement Actions and Workflow-Powered Rules
text:
Workflow‑powered rules are available to IAM customers who have both Workflow Builder and Agreement Actions. Only new rules created for Archive and Export actions will be workflow-powered. Legacy Agreement rules that existed **before** this feature was released are not changed. They continue to run on the existing Agreement Actions infrastructure.

You must have administrator permissions to create and edit Agreement rules and turn a rule into a workflow.

title: Agreement Actions and Workflow-Powered Rules
text:
Workflow-powered rules are a new Docusign IAM offering, so there are some significant limitations. The scope and function of these Agreement Action rules will expand over time. For now, please keep these limitations in mind as you explore this new feature:

- **Only Archive to rules are supported.** Other rule actions, such as Create Another Envelope, are not supported at this time.
- If an Agreement Actions Archive to rule is specified with any of the following conditions, the rule will be a legacy-style rule and not workflow-powered. You cannot use this type of rule to create a workflow.

  - **Archive to CLM.** Specifies archiving to CLM.
  - Customized envelope setting that specifies combining all envelope documents into a ZIP file but excludes the Certificate of Completion.

title: Agreement Actions and Workflow-Powered Rules
text:
Workflow-powered rules generate activity logs that capture key details such as the envelope acted upon, action type, execution status, and the date and time of execution.

These logs are accessible from the Agreement Rules list and are stored separately from other logs.

For more detailed instructions, refer to the topic [Access Rule Logs for Agreement Rules](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=tsk1244004b-76be-4f59-b82e-08bbc5700972.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to access execution logs for agreement rules used to trigger a Workflow Builder process.").

title: Risk Assessment Step
text:
Read about the Risk Assessment step, a Workflow Builder workflow
component that integrates dynamic participant risk signals to enable dynamic, data-driven
workflow orchestration and simplified compliance.

Risk Assessment is a step within a workflow that allows
process builders to integrate dynamic participant risk signals into their processes.
This step orchestrates intelligent workflows based on risk signals instead of static
assumptions. Participants submit personal information, such as their email address or
the last 4 digits of their Social Security number (SSN). The Docusign
trusted partner (Socure) then performs risk assessments and assigns participants a risk
score.

Process builders can retrieve Risk Assessment data from ID Evidence. They can also define subsequent steps for participants, based on their risk score.

title: Risk Assessment Step
text:
Process builders can use Risk Assessment steps across the
following business scenarios:

- Financial account opening: Financial services use the Risk
  Assessment step to conduct compliance-driven identity
  verification during account creation processes.
- Loan and Credit Approval Workflows: The Risk Assessment
  process dynamically verifies borrowers' identity based on risk profiles.
- Insurance Claims Processing: Claimants proceed with the Risk
  Assessment step that streamlines or intensifies verification
  based on user risk.
- High-value Transactions: Risk Assessment dynamically
  tailors verification intensity based on transactional risk. Low-risk
  participants benefit from low-friction verification processes, while high-risk
  participants require more thorough checks.

title: Risk Assessment Step
text:
When process builders add a Risk Assessment step to their
workflow, they must select the participant data to request to determine the risk
profile. For example, they can decide that each selected participant must submit
their phone number. Next, process builders select the data they want to store in ID
Evidence and the participants whose data they want to assess.

When participants enter the Risk Assessment step, they submit
the requested data, such as their phone number or address. The trusted partner
analyzes the submitted data to determine the appropriate risk level. For example, if
participants provide their phone number, the trusted partner returns a "Phone number
risk" variable. The score of this variable determines the risk level based on the
number.

Based on all the participants' data, the Risk Assessment step
returns a risk category (high, medium, low), a numerical score, and detailed
explanations.

When participants complete the Risk Assessment step, they
proceed to the subsequent steps based on their risk score. For example, process
builders can decide that participants with a high-risk profile must submit their ID
with liveness verification to verify their identity. They can also redirect
participants with a medium-risk profile to Knowledge-based authentication and let
participants with a low-risk profile proceed in the workflow.

Note: For more information about adding ID Verification and Knowledge-based authentication to a workflow, read the [Verify Someone's Identity Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=uak1735246809132.html&_LANG=enus "Learn about Verify Someone's Identity steps and how to use them to secure your automated agreement processes.") help page.

title: Risk Assessment Step
text:
The Risk Assessment step is only available to U.S.-based
participants. To add a Risk Assessment step, process builders must meet the
following criteria:

- Their account must enable Risk Assessment
- They must be an account administrator or a user with Workflows:
  Create permissions.

title: Risk Assessment Step
text:
If the Risk Assessment step configuration allows data storage,
Workflow Builder generates an ID Evidence file upon completion of the step. To retrieve the
ID Evidence information, process builders must add a Store
files step after the Risk Assessment step.
For more information, read the [ID Evidence in Workflow Builder](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=kmm1749205469137.html&_LANG=enus "Read about ID Evidence integration with Workflow Builder and how it helps deliver evidence of the ID verification process.") page.

title: Risk Assessment Step
heading_path: Risk Assessment step vs. Risk-Based Verification
text:
The Risk Assessment step and the ID Verification
Risk-Based Verification (RBV) method allow assessment of
participant risk. However, they both differ in scope and functionality. While
Risk Assessment is a Workflow Builder process step,
Risk-Based Verification is an ID Verification method tied
to an eSignature envelope. Risk Assessment only computes each
participant's risk score. Risk-Based Verification computes a
risk score and triggers a specific ID Verification configuration based on it.

While Risk Assessment does not include identity verification,
process builders can use participants' risk scores to define subsequent workflow
steps, including the Verify Someone's Identity step.

Both the Risk Assessment step and the ID Verification
Risk-Based Verification (RBV) allow storage of
participants' data in ID Evidence.

title: Understand Manual and Automatic Sending and Sender Options
heading_path: Understand Manual and Automatic Sending and Sender Options
text:
Learn about manual and automatic sending options for envelopes in your workflows, including how to choose who appears as the sender.

With the Send Documents for Signature step in certain workflows, process builders can specify two important features around how the envelope is sent. These settings determine **how the envelope is dispatched** and **whose name and email the recipient sees as the sender**.

Important: These sending options apply only to workflows that use either the Manually in Docusign or an API Call start method.

![Example of Automatic sending settings using the user who starts the workflow as the sender.](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/automatic_sending_settings.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU3ODQsInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.53UGErfpMke-sk6gEgZluIw0tRs3yEHKZKFsQAy3eAQ&_LANG=enus)

title: Understand Manual and Automatic Sending and Sender Options
heading_path: Understand Manual and Automatic Sending and Sender Options
text:
For workflows that use either the Manually in Docusign or an API start method, the process builder chooses how to send the eSignature envelope:

Automatically
:   When the workflow reaches the Send Documents for Signature step, the envelope is sent automatically.

    By default, the sender is the person who last published the workflow. However, the process builder can specify that the person who starts the workflow is the sender. This provides flexibility for who is responsible for sending the document.

Manually
:   When the workflow reaches the Send Documents for Signature step, the envelope goes through a required review and send step. The person who starts the workflow can review and edit all envelope details and the envelope is sent from the user's Docusign account.

Figure 1. Configuring sending options

![Side-by-side images showing automatic and manual sending settings.](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/auto-manual-sending-options.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU3ODQsInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.53UGErfpMke-sk6gEgZluIw0tRs3yEHKZKFsQAy3eAQ&_LANG=enus)

title: Understand Manual and Automatic Sending and Sender Options
heading_path: Understand Manual and Automatic Sending and Sender Options
text:
Using these sending options provides significant advantages in personalization and error prevention:

| Benefit | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Trusted sender experience | The envelope arrives from a known contact (the initiating user), rather than an automated workflow system. This personalization is vital for closing deals faster, adding assurance that the document came from a trusted source. |
| Final quality check | Manual sending provides an opportunity for a crucial, hands-on checkpoint to ensure all recipient details and any custom fields are accurate immediately before the document is sent for signature. |
| User familiarity | This experience makes the Send Document for Signature step align more closely with the standard Docusign eSignature process that customers are already familiar with. |

title: Workflow Builder Activity Logs
text:
Read about activity logs for workflows. Account Administrators, workflow administrators, process builders, and preparers can use these logs for a full audit trail of actions on workflow definitions and instances.

Activity Logs provide full visibility into all actions taken across your workflows, enabling better governance, simplified troubleshooting, and clear accountability for all workflow-related tasks. Activity logs are available at the account, workflow, or instance level and can be exported as a CSV. Customers use audit capabilities to check the status of a workflow or confirm that a workflow was executed as expected.

![](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/activity_log_for_workflow.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU5MjIsInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.2R3kRxTSPuzGQhuuaxWTknuM0CSkjAMKqf7-CRCNTYs&_LANG=enus)

title: Workflow Builder Activity Logs
heading_path: Video: View Activity Logs in Workflow Builder
text: Here's a short video to help you understand how to view and use workflow activity logs.

title: Workflow Builder Activity Logs
text:
The activity logs provide users with details about events for both workflow definitions and individual instances.

- **Comprehensive Tracking**: Logs capture a wide range of activities, including user actions, system events, and workflow configuration changes.
- **Filtering and Sorting**: Quickly locate specific events or actions based on criteria such as user, date, event type, or resource.
- **Audit Trail**: Maintains a secure and immutable record of all activities for compliance and auditing purposes.
- **Exporting**: Logs can be exported for further analysis.

title: Workflow Builder Activity Logs
text:
- **Security Auditing**: Track user access and changes to sensitive data or configurations.
- **Troubleshooting**: Identify the root cause of errors or unexpected behavior by reviewing recent activities.
- **Compliance**: Demonstrate adherence to regulatory requirements by providing a detailed activity history.
- **Operational Insights**: Analyze usage patterns and system performance over time.

title: Connect to Any Service Using Web Requests
heading_path: Connect to Any Service Using Web Requests
text:
Docusign Workflow Builder allows you to trigger external APIs using the Make a Web Request utility step.

The Make a Web Request step lets you send HTTP requests (GET, POST, or PUT) to almost any external or internal service as part of a Workflow Builder process, without waiting for a dedicated connector or app. This makes it easier to connect your agreements to systems such as CRMs, procurement tools, risk-scoring services, and internal APIs that are not yet available as first-class integrations.

title: Connect to Any Service Using Web Requests
heading_path: Connect to Any Service Using Web Requests
What the Web Request step does
text:
When you add a Make a Web Request step to a workflow, Workflow Builder can:

- Call an HTTP endpoint that you specify, using a supported method (**GET**, **POST**, or **PUT**).
- Pass data from earlier steps (such as participant inputs, agreement metadata, or values returned by other steps) into the request URL, headers, or body.
- Capture the response for GET requests and expose it as new variables you can reference in later steps, including for routing and branching decisions.

You can think of the Web Request step as a universal connector: Technical teams configure the underlying HTTP behavior. Process builders simply choose the step, select the right connection, and map which workflow data should be sent and received.

title: Connect to Any Service Using Web Requests
heading_path: Connect to Any Service Using Web Requests
text:
Use a Web Request step when you need Workflow Builder to talk to another system in the middle of a process. Examples include:

- Looking up data in a system of record and using that data to drive downstream steps. For example, pulling customer, pricing, or risk data from a CRM or internal API.
- Updating an external system when an agreement reaches a certain point. For example, pushing status updates into ServiceNow, Coupa, SAP Ariba, or another workflow tool.
- Calling Docusign CLM or other Docusign APIs from within a workflow, so you can orchestrate agreements alongside existing CLM processes.

Because the step is generic, the same configuration pattern can support many different APIs and business processes over time.

title: Connect to Any Service Using Web Requests
heading_path: Connect to Any Service Using Web Requests
How it fits into Workflow Builder
text:
The Web Request step is a first-party processing step in [Workflow Builder](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=uhm1697826856678.html&_LANG=enus "Learn the basics of how to create an agreement workflow. Process creators and account administrators can create workflows to automate the agreement process."), alongside steps that collect data, prepare documents, and send envelopes. Once enabled for your account, it is listed in the Utility section of the step selector. You can add it to any eligible workflow without installing a separate app.

From a process builder’s perspective, using the step typically involves three high-level decisions:

1. Choose which Web Request connection to use. For example, a connection that your administrator set up for a specific API.
2. Specify what the step should send. This includes the HTTP method, URL pattern, and which workflow variables to include in the headers or body.
3. Decide how to use the response. For example, mapping returned fields into workflow variables, or driving branches based on the status or content of the response.

title: Connect to Any Service Using Web Requests
heading_path: Connect to Any Service Using Web Requests
text:
The HTTP Web Request Extension is a premium capability and is available to customers on qualifying Docusign Intelligent Agreement Management (IAM) plans such as IAM Professional and above. The HTTP app is auto-installed for all authorized accounts and does not require manual installation from the [Docusign App Center](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=ous1698169987748&topicId=ijy1698170251179.html).

Before process builders use the step, an administrator or technical owner should configure at least one Web Request connection. This step currently supports Basic Auth (user name/password) connections. The connection defines the credentials to use for the API the step will call. At runtime, the Web Request step uses the credentials you selected and injects them into the request header.

Important: Connection credentials are securely stored and not exposed in workflows using them in a Web Request step. The workflow contains only an ID that is used to retrieve the credentials at runtime.

title: Mapping Data From Web Forms
text:
Learn the structural requirements and formatting rules for mapping advanced data types, such as checkboxes, radio groups, and dropdowns, from a web form to an agreement template.

For a Send Documents for Signature step, you use a prepared template or generated document to create an envelope. As an option, you can map data fields from other sources to prepopulate the document fields. This mapping is done in the Prepare eSignature Template or Prepare Document Template step. This data can be strings for values like text, dates, email addresses, and phone numbers, and also numerical values. You can also map data to these advanced field types: checkbox, radio group, and dropdown fields.

For the advanced field types, to map data between a web form and an eSignature template or generated document, there are some specific requirements. The web form API value must match the eSignature *value* property exactly. In addition, the web form API value does not allow spaces. You can use underscores or other characters to separate words for these values.

title: Mapping Data From Web Forms
text:
This example illustrates how you would prepare a radio group field to allow data mapping between a web form and an eSignature template.

In this example, there is a radio group field in an eSignature template for marital status. The Radio Button Values property uses underscores to concatenate the option text.

![Screenshot of a radio group field showing the Radio Button Values property.](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/radiobuttonvalues.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU3MzUsInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.dsPNoZnItU9QI-7XCp3375EODTAgpu31iBBE63jn814&_LANG=enus "Screenshot of a radio group field showing the Radio Button Values property.")

In the web form used to gather data for the eSignature document, the filing status question uses a Radio group. The API values for the radio options match the Radio Button Values from the eSignature template.

Note: Web form API values automatically concatenate the Option text with underscores ("\_"). You can edit the API values to match whatever values are used in the eSignature template.

![](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/webformradiooptions.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU3MzUsInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.dsPNoZnItU9QI-7XCp3375EODTAgpu31iBBE63jn814&_LANG=enus)

title: Mapping Data From Web Forms
heading_path: Admin setting for mapped web form data visibility
text:
When you map data from a web form to fields in an eSignature template, an account-level admin setting controls whether those mapped values are visible to all recipients when the envelope is sent.

When the **When an envelope is sent, write the initial value of the field for all recipients** setting is turned off, mapped values only appear to the recipient who is assigned those fields. Other recipients do not see the values until that recipient completes their fields and finishes signing.

To make mapped values visible to all recipients at send time, your Docusign administrator must turn on this setting in the **Admin** panel under **Sending Settings**. For more information about this setting and its effects, see [Docusign Support Center.](https://support.docusign.com/s/articles/How-do-I-share-the-information-in-all-fields-with-all-recipients-when-sending-a-document?language=en_US)

Note:

This setting does not affect document templates or generated documents.

title: Access and Permissions for Extension App Connections
heading_path: Access and Permissions for Extension App Connections
text:
Outlines the access levels and permissions required to edit or publish workflow steps that use private or shared extension application connections.

Docusign and Workflow administrators can manage all workflows on an account. However, if a workflow includes an extension app step that uses a private connection, specific **connection guardrails** apply. These guardrails protect the private connection data while allowing the administrator to still manage and publish the overall workflow. Configurator details for private connections remain locked and hidden from users who don't own the connection — protecting sensitive resources.

While the administrator cannot view or edit the locked configuration of a private connection that they do not own, they maintain full oversight of the process. This topic explores these access levels and permissions for workflows that include third-party (3P) extension app connections in more detail.

title: Access and Permissions for Extension App Connections
heading_path: Access and Permissions for Extension App Connections
text:
Workflow steps for extension applications can be configured to use either a private or shared connection to a third-party application. The connection type affects who can configure the step.

Shared connection
:   Users with the **Workflows: Manage** permission can edit the step configuration for the extension application.

Private connection
:   Only the connection owner can edit the step configuration. For all other users, including those with the **Workflows: Manage** permission, the step will display in a locked or restricted state. These users cannot view or edit the step configuration or make any changes to the connection.

    These users can still:

    - Edit other steps in the workflow (for example, Web forms, confirmation screens).
    - Delete the extension application step.
    - Contact the private 3P app connection owner to have them make changes to the step.
    - Run the workflow.

    ![Example of the locked state of a Read from Salesforce step that uses a private connection.](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/connection_error_example.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU4OTcsInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.6mN4en0T5HRDNFc1IC3ywLsB5pb-7nEVeVcPB-v7Odc&_LANG=enus "Example of the locked state of a Read from Salesforce step that uses a private connection.")

title: Access and Permissions for Extension App Connections
heading_path: Access and Permissions for Extension App Connections
text:
Users with the **Workflows: Manage** permission can publish a workflow even if it contains steps with private connections they do not own. Multiple users can contribute to and publish a single workflow, even if it contains steps tied to different private third-party accounts (for example, Salesforce or Google Drive).

- When a connection owner adds a private 3P account to a workflow step, they **authorize** the workflow to use their connection credentials. This authorization remains valid even if another user publishes the workflow.
- While any workflow manager can publish the workflow, the private configuration details (such as API keys or login credentials) remain locked and hidden from everyone except the original connection owner.
- No single user must own all connections in a workflow to publish it.

title: Access and Permissions for Extension App Connections
heading_path: Access and Permissions for Extension App Connections
text: Any user with the **Workflows Manage**, **Create**, or **Prepare** permission can start an instance of a published workflow, even if it includes steps with private or shared connections to third-party extension apps. The connection type does not restrict the ability to trigger a workflow.

title: Configure a Prepare Document Template Step
heading_path: Configure a Prepare Document Template Step
text: Learn how to incorporate a document template into your process to generate customized agreements automatically using workflow variables.

title: Configure a Prepare Document Template Step
heading_path: Configure a Prepare Document Template Step
text:
- Recommended: Access to an existing document template to use for your workflow step. For more information about document templates, see the [Document Templates](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=map332c3ebc-8fca-4fac-9b9f-c7206adc3985&topicId=conc8f06624-6ef6-40a3-bbc8-c5c7fe18746b.html&_LANG=enus) guide.

Use the Prepare Document Template step to prepare customized agreements automatically using workflow variables and document templates. While this step is often used for dynamic customization, it also supports static documents.

**Key Capabilities:**

- **Dynamic Content:** Uses conditional logic to show or hide content based on mapped values. For more information about how you can use conditional logic with document templates, see [this help topic](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?topicId=ziz1707785438108.html&_LANG=enus).
- **Dual Mapping:** Map workflow data to both **Sender** fields (prepopulated as part of the generated document) and **Recipient** fields (assigned to a signer).
- **Static Document Support:** Your document template does not require fields. You can use this step to include standard, non-customized files in your agreement process. Static documents are available for subsequent steps like Send Documents for Signature.
- **Multi-Document Support:** Each step generates **one** document. You can add multiple Prepare Document Template steps to a single workflow to generate multiple files, and send them all together in one Send Documents for Signature step.

**Mapping Your Data**

When configuring this step, you can map workflow variables to these field types:

| **Field type** | **Description** |
| --- | --- |
| **Sender Fields** | Data prepopulated from prior steps (like a web form). Editable by the reviewer participant in a manual review, allowing for a quality control check before the process continues. Note: Include sender fields in your template to use the **manual review** feature. If the template lacks these fields, the workflow cannot support a manual review. |
| **Recipient Fields** | Placeholders for information the signer must provide or verify. Mapping data here gives them a "head start" on their form. |

Note: If using a static document, data mapping is skipped as there are no fields to prepopulate.

A typical workflow starts with a Collect Data with Web Forms step to gather details from your user. Next, the Prepare Document Template step takes that information and uses it to complete your document automatically. Once the document is ready, a Send Documents for Signature step routes it to your signers.

![Screenshot of example workflow for a Prepare Document Template step.](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/doc-gen-example.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU3MzksInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.QdJrWEASqbkGeZ_pzfz8TWKkzHeVbJudjnskQIROKRE&_LANG=enus)

1. Create a workflow as described in [Create a Basic Workflow](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=uhm1697826856678.html&_LANG=enus "Learn the basics of how to create an agreement workflow. Process creators and account administrators can create workflows to automate the agreement process.").
2. Add the steps that you want to precede your Prepare Document Template step, such as a Collect Data with Web Forms step.
3. Select Add a step >  Prepare Document Template >  Configure.
4. Select the document template to use for the step.
5. If your workflow start method is Manually in Docusign or From an API Call, select whether you want to include a manual review of the generated document. This review as a quality control measure. The workflow is paused after the document is generated to allow the reviewer to inspect it, and potentially edit values.

title: Configure a Prepare Document Template Step
heading_path: Configure a Prepare Document Template Step
text:
ual review of the generated document. This review as a quality control measure. The workflow is paused after the document is generated to allow the reviewer to inspect it, and potentially edit values.

   Note: You can only use manual review for document templates that contain **sender fields**. If your selected template lacks sender fields, the system disables this option.

   1. Toggle the option to No to turn on manual review.
   2. Select the workflow participant who will complete the review.

      ![Manual review options for a Prepare Document Template step.](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/manual_review_select_reviewer.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU3MzksInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.QdJrWEASqbkGeZ_pzfz8TWKkzHeVbJudjnskQIROKRE&_LANG=enus)
   3. By default, the reviewer is permitted to edit sender field data for the document. Clear this option if you want to restrict editing.
6. Map workflow variables fields to the template's fields. You can map variables to two types of template fields:

   - **Sender fields:** Prepopulates data that becomes part of the document. These fields can be reviewed during a manual review.
   - **Recipient fields:** Prepopulates input fields for the signer, which they can see or edit during the signing session.You can select variables from any prior step, such as a web form. Fields with a red asterisk require a mapping. Map variables to optional fields as needed.

   Note: If a document template sender field was used in a prior step, it will be automatically mapped for you.

   ![](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/map_fields_dt.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU3MzksInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.QdJrWEASqbkGeZ_pzfz8TWKkzHeVbJudjnskQIROKRE&_LANG=enus)
7. Specify a name and file format for your generated document. Adding some type of unique identifier to the document name helps avoid creating multiple documents with the same or similar names.
   1. Use the title of the document template or select workflow variables to customize the name of each generated document. If you use variables, the title of the generated document is dynamically constructed using the specified variables. These variables are concatenated with underscores to form the final filename.
   2. Select the file format. Options: .docx; .pdf

      ![](https://docusign-be-prod.zoominsoftware.io/api/bundle/yff1696971835267/page/images/doc-gen-file-name.png?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJjdXN0b21lciI6ImRvY3VzaWduX3Byb2R1Y3Rpb24iLCJleHAiOjE3ODE4MDU3MzksInNoZWFmIjoieWZmMTY5Njk3MTgzNTI2NyJ9.QdJrWEASqbkGeZ_pzfz8TWKkzHeVbJudjnskQIROKRE&_LANG=enus)
   3. Select Apply.

The Generate Document step is added to your workflow and configured. You can use the generated document in subsequent workflow steps, such as a Send Documents for Signature step.

title: I Cannot Find the Identity Verification Configuration I Want for a Verify Someone's Identity Step
heading_path: I Cannot Find the Identity Verification Configuration I Want for a Verify Someone's Identity Step
text:
Troubleshoot why a specific identity verification configuration does not appear in your drop-down menu when configuring a
Verify Someone's Identity step in Workflow Builder.

I can't see the identity verification configuration I want in the Identity
Verification Configuration drop-down list of the Verify
Someone’s Identity step. Although this configuration exists in the
Identity Verification settings, I can’t find a way to use it in my workflow.

The identity verification configuration you want to add to your Verify
Someone’s Identity step has the following characteristics:

- It is an ID Verification for EU Qualified
  configuration or a custom configuration based on ID Verification
  for EU Qualified
- It includes the Manually review the ID document
  option
- It includes the Allow SMS authentication after one successful
  Photo ID or Passport verification option

Any identity verification configuration that includes either of those
characteristics is not supported in Workflow Builder and cannot, therefore, appear in the
Identity Verification Configuration drop-down list of
the Verify Someone’s Identity step.

You can remove these non-compliant
options from your identity verification configuration or choose a configuration
that does not include them.

For more information on modifying configurations,
see the topic [Edit Identity Verification Configurations](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=pik1583277475390&topicId=uwh1583277457043.html&_LANG=enus)

title: Capture ID Evidence Data
text: Learn how to configure your workflow to enable ID Evidence to capture data.

title: Capture ID Evidence Data
text:
To capture ID Evidence data from a workflow step, you must follow these prerequisites:

- You must use either a Verify Someone’s Identity or a
  Risk Assessment step whose configuration enables data
  storage. For more information about ID Evidence limitations in Workflow Builder, read the
  [ID Evidence in
  Workflow Builder](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=kmm1749205469137.html&_LANG=enus "Read about ID Evidence integration with Workflow Builder and how it helps deliver evidence of the ID verification process.") page.
- You are a Docusign administrator or a workflow creator as specified in the [Workflow Builder Roles and Permission Requirements](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=gbb1696973048215.html&_LANG=enus "Read about the different roles involved with creating and using workflows with Docusign Workflow Builder. Each role requires specific user permissions.") page.
- You have installed and authorized the application that stores ID Evidence data on your account. Read the [Install Apps in the Docusign App Center](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=ous1698169987748&topicId=ctg1698170340729.html&_LANG=enus) and [ID Evidence in Workflow Builder](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=kmm1749205469137.html&_LANG=enus "Read about ID Evidence integration with Workflow Builder and how it helps deliver evidence of the ID verification process.") pages for authorized applications to store ID Evidence files.

As a process builder, you want to capture data from a Verify Someone’s
Identity or a Risk Assessment step. For
example, you want to capture the names of the participants who verified their
identity and the verification process results. You may also want to capture a copy
of the identity document participants used to identify themselves. Once ID Evidence
has captured the data, you want to access it through a PDF file in a specific
repository.

1. Create a new workflow or edit an existing workflow.
2. Ensure that your workflow includes a Verify Someone’s
   Identity or Risk Assessment step.

   If your workflow does not include such steps, add one as described in the
   [Configure a Verify
   Someone's Identity Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=qht1698126463108.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to configure a Verify Someone's Identity step in an agreement workflow. You use an identity verification step to verify a participant's identity using Docusign's ID Verification process.") or in the [Configure a Risk Assessment Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=tsk6ecf58be-0285-4608-92cd-66252f6e07a5.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to configure the Risk Assessment step in a workflow. This step gathers participant risk signals for dynamic orchestration, data-driven decisions, and simplified compliance.") page.

   Important: The identity verification configuration that you use in
   your Verify Someone’s Identity or Risk
   Assessment step configuration must allow data
   storage. For more information, read the [Add Identity Verification Configurations](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=pik1583277475390&topicId=dnh1583277456343.html&_LANG=enus) or the [Configure a Risk Assessment Step](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=tsk6ecf58be-0285-4608-92cd-66252f6e07a5.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to configure the Risk Assessment step in a workflow. This step gathers participant risk signals for dynamic orchestration, data-driven decisions, and simplified compliance.") help pages.
3. Add other steps as needed to complete your workflow.
4. Select Add a step > Store files and choose the storage application where you want to archive your
   ID Evidence file.

   For example, Store files in Google Drive.

title: Capture ID Evidence Data
text:
eeded to complete your workflow.
4. Select Add a step > Store files and choose the storage application where you want to archive your
   ID Evidence file.

   For example, Store files in Google Drive.

   Workflow Builder adds your step to the workflow.
5. Select Configure.

   Workflow Builder opens the configuration panel.
6. Configure your Store files step.

   Important: Ensure you select ID Evidence file in the Select a file or envelope from any earlier step in this workflow list.

   For more information about configuring a Store files step, read the following pages:

   - [Configure a Google Store Files Step in an Agreement Workflow](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=wqt1698167316926.html&_LANG=enus "Learn how to configure a Google Drive storage step to automatically archive documents generated in earlier workflow steps.")
   - [Configure a Dropbox Store Files Step in an Agreement Workflow](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=ous1698169987748&topicId=rgp1706110555492.html&_LANG=enus)
   - [Configure a SharePoint Store Files Step in an Agreement Workflow](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=ous1698169987748&topicId=haj1706107382131.html&_LANG=enus)
   - [Configure a Microsoft OneDrive File Archive Step in an Agreement Workflow](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=ous1698169987748&topicId=wir1706111649199.html&_LANG=enus)
   - [Configure a Box Store Files Step in an Agreement Workflow](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=ous1698169987748&topicId=dfb1706110409817.html&_LANG=enus)
7. Select Apply.
8. Select Review and Publish.

   The Review before publishing panel opens.
9. Review and publish your workflow as explained in the [Review and Publish a Workflow](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=yff1696971835267&topicId=iqm1698272226447.html&_LANG=enus "As a process builder, publishing a workflow validates your steps are properly configured and makes the workflow available to your account members. Once published, preparers can start instances of your workflow.") page.

title: Capture ID Evidence Data
text: Your workflow is now ready for use. ID Evidence will capture participants’ data and export it to the appropriate directory. Open this directory to access the PDF export file.

title: Docusign Workflow Builder Overview
text:
Read about the new Docusign platform for automating agreements.
Customize, connect, and automate your agreement workflows with Docusign Workflow Builder.

title: Docusign Workflow Builder Overview
text:
A typical agreement process consists of numerous steps to complete before and after
signing the agreement. Some of these steps can be addressed today by integrating
multiple Docusign products, usually with expert assistance and developer resources.

Now Docusign Workflow Builder offers a platform to integrate Docusign products and is extensible to third-party products.
Through this new platform, customers can build a variety of business processes
without writing code and digitize their agreement journeys end-to-end.

Docusign Workflow Builder is a core platform service powering the Docusign IAM portfolio. Easily build and deploy customized
workflows that automate and accelerate your agreement processes without writing any
code. Workflow Builder connects all the tools and activities in your workflow so agreement
processes are more efficient, more uniform, and have better visibility.

With Docusign Workflow Builder, you can combine workflow steps for Docusign apps like ID Verification, Web Forms, and eSignature, and
third-party extensions to automate your agreement process end-to-end.

The topics in this guide help process builders and preparers with how to create, use,
and manage workflows.

title: Docusign Workflow Builder Overview
text:
The following resources are available for developers as part of the Workflow Builder API
to build workflows and extension apps. If you would like to explore our developer tools for Workflow Builder, here are some resources to help you get started.

[Workflow Builder API](https://developers.docusign.com/docs/workflow-builder-api/)
:   Create and manage workflows using the Workflow Builder API.

[Extension Apps](https://developers.docusign.com/extension-apps/)
:   Build custom third-party functionality into Docusign and
    publish your solutions in the App Center.

[Workflows Sample App](https://mymaestro.sampleapps.docusign.com/)
:   Discover the power of the Docusign Workflow Builder API.
    Create, trigger, and manage workflows in an interactive environment.

title: Workflow Builder Roles and Permission Requirements
heading_path: Workflow Builder Roles and Permission Requirements
text:
Read about the different roles involved with creating and using workflows with Docusign Workflow Builder. Each role requires specific user
permissions.

Using Workflow Builder to create workflows and automate your agreement processes involves
different roles and responsibilities for your organization. For you to participate in
creating or using workflows, you must have the appropriate user permissions assigned to
you by your account administrator. Administrators can find information on assigning
permissions in the [Permission Profiles](https://support.docusign.com/s/document-item?bundleId=pik1583277475390&topicId=pof1583277362435.html&_LANG=enus) article.

Docusign administrator
:   The administrator for a Docusign IAM account authorizes
    applications for Workflow Builder and enables users to create and run workflows by
    granting required user permissions. Account administrators are granted full
    workflow permissions by default. Minimum required permissions are listed for
    each user role.

Workflow administrator
:   The workflow administrator is a Docusign user who can view, edit, publish, trigger, export, and audit all workflows across the account, regardless of who originally created them. This role is designed for users who need centralized oversight and management of workflows without being the original workflow creator.

    All Docusign administrators inherit the Manage permission by default. Administrators can also assign this permission to other users or groups. With this role, non-administrators can manage all workflows across the account like a Docusign administrator, but without granting them full administrative privileges.
:   Required user permissions:

    - Workflows: Manage
    - Allow user to send envelopes: enabled
    - Web Forms: Create
    - Templates: Create

    Note: If a workflow step uses a extension app with a third-party connection that is private and the workflow administrator is not the connection owner, they cannot edit the step.
